questions.text: I tend to experience emotions as informational signals rather than as overwhelming forces.
I find that when I shop for myself, I end up buying things for other people instead.
I am comfortable standing alone in my position if I think it is right.
I believe the most efficient communication is the most literal.
I can tell when someone is narrating their own actions aloud to shape how others perceive what they are doing.
I find that a day with too many emotional inputs leaves me feeling physically ill.
I believe that a focused pursuit of personal excellence makes life meaningful.
I keep a master list of all ongoing projects and their current statuses.
I prefer to be a contributor alongside others rather than the sole architect.
I find that the felt weight of another person's suffering is the experience that has done the most to awaken my sense of moral urgency.
I find that a calm, objective stance during others' crises allows me to be a stabilizing force when one is needed most.
I find that analytical clarity during times of distress has made me a more strategic thinker and a more dependable partner in collaborative problem-solving.
I believe that the trajectory of my career is the single most important line on my personal graph.
I prefer it when people say exactly what they mean.
I use a decision journal to record significant choices and revisit them later.
I regularly clean out expired items from my refrigerator and pantry.
I feel that my willingness to let go of control in uncertain moments is what makes the group effective.
I find that measuring progress too closely can kill the creative spark.
I feel that keeping my accomplishments quiet so others can feel proud of theirs is a worthy trade.
I believe that the felt weight of suffering is the moral experience that most urgently calls me to act.
I thrive when I can respond to whatever the moment presents rather than sticking to a predetermined path.
I can tell when two people have had a private conversation before a group meeting based on how they interact.
I consider it part of my job to support others by walking alongside their growth.
I routinely put community obligations above personal recreation.
I think best while doing, not while planning on paper.
I feel drawn to positions of formal authority.
I hold the view that maintaining rational focus during emotionally intense events is a deliberate practice that yields better judgment with each iteration.
I believe that the moral urgency of suffering is a signal I have learned to take seriously, and I would not want to lose that capacity.
I believe that my low emotional reactivity is an authentic expression of who I am, not suppression.
I notice that my nervous system keeps a running tally of unresolved emotions, and the total has physical weight.
I am the person who sets the agenda for one-on-one conversations.
I rarely sense the invisible boundaries that others seem to navigate naturally in social spaces.
I pick up on the subtle power plays that happen in everyday interactions.
I find that owning the best version of something, even when a cheaper option would do, reflects the standard I hold for myself.
I find that the act of saying goodbye activates a cascade of feeling that takes hours to process.
I find that the highs of my life are pleasant and satisfying without being ecstatic.
